content.problemStatement:
You work as an engineer. You were given an empty board with KKK consecutive cells; at any moment, each cell can display one character.

You want the board to display a string SSS with length N>KN > KN>K. Since the board isn't large enough, you want to display the string in N−K+1N-K+1N−K+1 steps. In the iii-th step (1≤i≤N−K+11 \le i \le N-K+11≤i≤N−K+1), you'll make the board display the characters Si,Si+1,…,Si+K−1S_i, S_{i+1}, \dots, S_{i+K-1}Si​,Si+1​,…,Si+K−1​.

The power required to switch the board from step iii to step i+1i+1i+1 (for 1≤i≤N−K1 \le i \le N-K1≤i≤N−K) is equal to the number of characters displayed on the board that have to change between these steps. You should find the total power required for the whole process of displaying a string, i.e. the sum of powers required for switching between all consecutive pairs of steps.
constraints: each character of S is a lowercase English letter
the sum of N for all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and K. The second line contains a single string S with length N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the total power required for text switching.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: For the first test case, the power required to switch the board from step 1 to step 2 is 1, from step 2 to step 3 is 2, and from step 3 to step 4 is 1. Therefore, the total power required is 1 + 2 + 1 = 4.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by iterating over the string S and comparing the characters in the current window with the characters in the next window. The power required to switch the board from step i to step i+1 is equal to the number of characters that have to change between these steps. This can be calculated by comparing the characters at the corresponding positions in the two windows.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the length of the string S. The algorithm iterates over each test case and for each test case, it iterates over the string S.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — where N is the length of the string S. The algorithm uses a string of length N to store the input string S.
